name: Inventory Management Spring Boot Application
scenario: Build a Spring Boot application skeleton for an inventory management system. No REST endpoints yet — this is purely the service layer: Spring beans, dependency injection, properties configuration, and a CommandLineRunner that demonstrates the wired components working together.
description: A multi-service Spring Boot app: InventoryService, WarehouseService, and AlertService — all wired together with constructor injection. Configuration via application.properties. @PostConstruct initialization. CommandLineRunner demonstrates the full wiring on startup.

core.description: `Application.java` with @SpringBootApplication and `main()`. All other classes in sub-packages.
`InventoryRepository` (@Repository): in-memory Map<String, InventoryItem>. Methods: add(item), findBySku(sku) → Optional<InventoryItem>, findAll() → List, count() → int.
`InventoryService` (@Service): depends on InventoryRepository (constructor injection). Methods: addItem(sku, name, qty, price), getItem(sku), getAllItems(), getTotalValue() (sum of qty × price).
`AlertService` (@Service): reads @Value `inventory.low-stock-threshold` (int, default 10). Method isLowStock(InventoryItem) → qty < threshold. @PostConstruct prints threshold.
`InventoryStartupRunner` (@Component implements CommandLineRunner): depends on InventoryService and AlertService. In run(): adds 3 test items, prints all items, total value, flags low-stock items.

bonus.description: Add Spring profiles: application-dev.properties (threshold=2, DEBUG logging) vs application-prod.properties (threshold=20, WARN logging). Demonstrate profile activation.
Add a @SpringBootTest integration test (WebEnvironment.NONE) that autowires InventoryService, adds items, and asserts getTotalValue() returns the correct sum.
Add @ConfigurationProperties(prefix='inventory') as an alternative to multiple @Value fields. Create an InventoryProperties class with lowStockThreshold, maxItems, and currency fields.

data_to_use:
Test inventory items:
SKU-001: 'Wireless Mouse', qty=50, price=₹799
SKU-002: 'Mechanical Keyboard', qty=3, price=₹1999 (low stock!)
SKU-003: 'USB-C Hub', qty=8, price=₹499 (low stock at threshold=5? No. At threshold=10? Yes!)

Expected output on startup:
=== Inventory System Started ===
Alert: threshold=5
Items: 3
  SKU-001: Wireless Mouse | qty:50 | ₹799 | [OK]
  SKU-002: Keyboard | qty:3 | ₹1999 | [LOW STOCK!]
  SKU-003: USB Hub | qty:8 | ₹499 | [OK]
Total inventory value: ₹47942
example_output:
.   ____          _            __ _ _
 /\\ / ___'_ __ _ _(_)_ __  __ _ \ \ \ \
...
INFO  - Started Application in 2.1 seconds
[AlertService] Low stock threshold: 5

=== Inventory System ===
SKU-001 | Wireless Mouse      | qty=50 | ₹799.00  [OK]
SKU-002 | Mechanical Keyboard | qty=3  | ₹1999.00 [LOW STOCK!]
SKU-003 | USB-C Hub           | qty=8  | ₹499.00  [OK]

Total items: 3
Total inventory value: ₹47942.00
Low stock alerts: 1
ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. Key: ALL classes must be in sub-packages of Application.java's package for @ComponentScan to find them. Constructor injection: no @Autowired annotation needed when there's only one constructor. getTotalValue(): items.stream().mapToDouble(i -> i.quantity() * i.price()).sum().
submission_instructions: Push to GitHub. Run with `mvn spring-boot:run`. Show startup output in README.
tips_for_success: Package all classes under com.codequest.inventory (or whatever you choose for Application)
InventoryRepository: private final Map<String, InventoryItem> store = new LinkedHashMap<>()
CommandLineRunner.run() is called after all beans are created — perfect for demo output
